threats.title: Complete Drain of Factory Asset Tokens via Custom Token Aliasing
threats.hypothesis_description: An attacker can drain all `assetToken` deposits from `AgentFactoryV4` by creating a custom token application where the `customToken` is set to the `assetToken` address, and then withdrawing it.
threats.investigation_rationale: The `initFromToken` function allows users to pass any address as `tokenAddr`. If an attacker passes the `assetToken` address, the factory records `assetToken` as the `customToken` for that application. When the attacker calls `withdraw`, the function first refunds the `applicationThreshold` (in `assetToken`), and then attempts to refund the custom token by transferring `IERC20(customToken).balanceOf(address(this))`. Since `customToken` is `assetToken`, this second transfer sends the factory's entire `assetToken` balance (including deposits from all other users) to the attacker.

proof_of_signal.pattern: withdraw transfers IERC20(customToken).balanceOf(address(this)) without verifying customToken != assetToken

preconditions: AgentFactoryV4 holds assetToken deposits from other active applications
Attacker has enough assetToken to cover applicationThreshold + initialLP (can be 1 wei)

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Aliasing customToken to assetToken causes the balance check to return the entire factory treasury
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker profit = Total factory assetToken balance - applicationThreshold

economic_questions: Can the attacker successfully pass the isCompatibleToken check with assetToken?
Does the factory hold sufficient funds to make this profitable after gas costs?
validation_requirements: Deploy AgentFactoryV4, deposit multiple applications, and attempt the aliasing attack in a local fork

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: Standard transaction gas for initFromToken and withdraw
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: All assetTokens held by the factory (potentially millions of dollars depending on TVL)
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Single block (two transactions or one atomic contract call)

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When the factory has maximum pending applications
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Protocol insolvency, complete loss of user deposits

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All users with pending applications in AgentFactoryV4

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify the exact sequence of transfers in withdraw

threats.title: Complete Governance Hijack via Arbitrary Delegation Overwrite
threats.hypothesis_description: An attacker can arbitrarily overwrite the voting delegation of any user by calling `stake` on their behalf with a minimal amount (e.g., 1 wei).
threats.investigation_rationale: The `stake` function in `AgentVeToken.sol` allows the caller to specify both the `receiver` of the minted veTokens and the `delegatee`. It then calls `_delegate(receiver, delegatee)`. Because `_delegate` updates the delegation for the entire balance of the `receiver`, an attacker can stake 1 wei of the asset token for a whale (the victim) and set the `delegatee` to the attacker's address. This instantly transfers the victim's entire voting power to the attacker without the victim's consent.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _delegate(receiver, delegatee) is called without verifying msg.sender == receiver

preconditions: Victim has a large balance of veTokens
Attacker has at least 1 wei of the staking assetToken

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: _delegate(receiver, delegatee) overrides previous delegation for the entire balance
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker forces delegation of victim's tokens to themselves
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker gains majority voting power, allowing them to pass malicious DAO proposals (e.g., changing the DAO address, draining treasury)

economic_questions: What is the economic value of controlling the Agent DAO?
Can the attacker execute a malicious proposal before the victim notices and re-delegates?
validation_requirements: Test the stake function by staking for another address and verifying their voting power shifts
Analyze the Agent DAO's capabilities to determine the maximum extractable value from a governance hijack

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: Low gas cost per stake call
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Depends on DAO treasury and control over agent metadata/services
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Single block to hijack, plus DAO voting period to execute malicious proposal

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Right before a critical DAO vote, or when victims are inactive
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Complete loss of governance integrity for the affected agent

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Loss of voting power and potential loss of agent value
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Total value controlled by the Agent DAO

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Check DAO voting period and threshold

threats.title: Systematic Extraction of Tax Revenue via Zero-Slippage Autoswap
threats.hypothesis_description: MEV bots can sandwich the automatic tax swap mechanism to extract nearly 100% of the protocol's tax revenue due to the absence of slippage protection.
threats.investigation_rationale: In `AgentToken.sol`, when the accumulated tax reaches the `swapThreshold`, the `_autoSwap` function is triggered during a user's transfer. This calls `_swapTax`, which executes a trade on Uniswap V2 using `swapExactTokensForTokensSupportingFeeOnTransferTokens` with `amountOutMin` hardcoded to `0`. Because there is no slippage protection, an MEV bot can observe the pending transaction, front-run it by heavily skewing the pool price, let the autoswap execute at a terrible rate, and then back-run it to restore the price, pocketing the tax value.
preconditions: Sufficient liquidity in the Uniswap pair to allow manipulation

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Sandwich attack on the Uniswap pool during the autoswap transaction
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker captures the value of the swapped tax tokens minus swap fees and gas

economic_questions: What is the typical swapThreshold value and how much value does it represent?
Is the Uniswap pool shallow enough to make the sandwich attack highly profitable?
validation_requirements: Calculate the exact MEV extraction potential based on swapThreshold and expected pool liquidity
Simulate a sandwich attack on the autoswap in a local fork

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Flash loan sufficient to move the Uniswap pool price significantly
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: Gas for front-run and back-run transactions + Uniswap 0.3% fees
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Up to 99% of the tax swap value per occurrence
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: High, as it can be executed risk-free via MEV bundles

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Requires flash loan liquidity for the pairToken
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Protocol treasury receives near-zero revenue from taxes

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Indirect impact via loss of protocol funding
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: 100% of generated tax revenue over time

threats.title: Validator Score Inflation and Sybil Attack via Permissionless Registration
threats.hypothesis_description: An attacker can arbitrarily inflate their validator score by registering multiple Sybil addresses as validators after a large number of proposals have been created.
threats.investigation_rationale: In `AgentNftV2.sol`, the `addValidator` function is public and lacks access control. When called, it triggers `_initValidatorScore`, which sets the validator's base score to `totalProposals(virtualId)`. If an agent has had 1,000 proposals, an attacker can create 100 new addresses, call `addValidator` for each, and instantly gain a combined base score of 100,000 without ever having participated in governance. This breaks the ELO/reputation system and allows the attacker to dominate any mechanisms relying on `validatorScore` or `totalUptimeScore`.

proof_of_signal.pattern: addValidator is public and calls _initValidatorScore which grants base score equal to totalProposals

preconditions: The targeted agent (virtualId) has a high number of past proposals

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Sybil registration of validators to accumulate unearned base scores
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker captures a disproportionate share of any rewards distributed based on validator score, or manipulates ELO-gated actions

economic_questions: Are protocol rewards or fees distributed proportionally to validatorScore?
Does a high validator score grant special privileges in the DAO or core upgrades?
validation_requirements: Trace all usages of validatorScore() and totalUptimeScore() across the ecosystem to quantify exact financial impact
Verify if the off-chain reward distribution relies on these on-chain scores

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Depends on the reward pool allocated to validators
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Can be executed in a single block via a smart contract

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: After an agent has accumulated many proposals
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Dilution of legitimate validator rewards to near zero

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Severe dilution of reputation and rewards

threats.title: Denial of Service via Unbounded Validator Loop
threats.hypothesis_description: An attacker can permanently disable functions relying on `totalUptimeScore` by registering thousands of Sybil validators, causing the function to exceed the block gas limit.
threats.investigation_rationale: The `totalUptimeScore` function in `ValidatorRegistry.sol` iterates over the entire `_validators[virtualId]` array to sum up scores. Because `addValidator` in `AgentNftV2.sol` is public and permissionless, an attacker can write a script or contract to add thousands of unique addresses as validators for a specific `virtualId`. Once the array is sufficiently large, any call to `totalUptimeScore` will revert with an Out of Gas (OOG) error, permanently bricking any on-chain or off-chain logic that depends on it.
preconditions: Attacker is willing to spend gas to register thousands of validators

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Array bloat leading to Out of Gas
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Primarily a griefing/DoS attack, though could be used to hold the protocol hostage or disrupt competitor agents

economic_questions: What critical protocol functions depend on totalUptimeScore?
Does the off-chain reward distributor call this function, and will it break the entire reward pipeline?
validation_requirements: Calculate the exact number of validators needed to hit the Base network block gas limit
Estimate the gas cost required for the attacker to execute the attack

preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: A few blocks to spam registrations

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Disruption of protocol operations and reward distributions

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Agent ecosystem relying on uptime scores

threats.title: Extraction of Excess Custom Tokens on Withdrawal
threats.hypothesis_description: If the factory holds excess balances of a custom token, a user withdrawing their application will receive the entire factory balance of that token, not just their deposit.
threats.investigation_rationale: In `AgentFactoryV4.sol`, when a user withdraws a custom token application, the contract executes `IERC20(customToken).safeTransfer(application.proposer, IERC20(customToken).balanceOf(address(this)))`. Instead of tracking the `initialLP` amount deposited by the user and returning exactly that amount, it sweeps the entire balance. If other users have accidentally sent tokens to the factory, or if multiple applications somehow use the same custom token (e.g., via the sentinel collision bug), the withdrawer steals those excess funds.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Transfers balanceOf(address(this)) instead of a tracked deposit amount

preconditions: Factory holds more customToken than the user deposited (via airdrops, accidental transfers, or duplicate applications)

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Withdrawing an application sweeps all tokens of that type
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker gains any excess tokens held by the factory

economic_questions: Is it common for the factory to hold excess custom tokens?
Can this be chained with the sentinel collision bug (INV-ACCESS-002) to steal other users' initialLP deposits?
validation_requirements: Test withdrawing an application after manually sending extra custom tokens to the factory

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Variable, depends on excess tokens in factory

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Loss of accidentally deposited funds or other users' deposits

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Users who accidentally transferred tokens, or other proposers

threats.title: Infinite Token Minting via Tax Rate Underflow
threats.hypothesis_description: If an admin sets the tax rate above 100%, the unchecked arithmetic in tax processing will underflow, minting near-infinite tokens to the recipient.
threats.investigation_rationale: In `AgentToken.sol`, `setProjectTaxRates` does not enforce an upper bound on `projectBuyTaxBasisPoints` or `projectSellTaxBasisPoints`. If an admin (or compromised admin key) sets the rate > 10,000 (100%), the calculated `tax` in `_taxProcessing` will be greater than `sentAmount_`. Because the subtraction `amountLessTax_ -= tax` occurs inside an `unchecked` block, it will underflow, resulting in `amountLessTax_` becoming a massive number (~uint256.max). This massive number is then added to the recipient's balance, effectively minting infinite tokens.

proof_of_signal.pattern: amountLessTax_ -= tax inside unchecked block with no upper bound on tax rate

preconditions: Admin or factory sets tax rate > 10000 basis points
A transfer occurs that triggers the tax

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Underflow in unchecked block causes massive balance increase
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Recipient gains near-infinite tokens, which can be dumped to drain the LP

economic_questions: Is there any off-chain safeguard preventing the admin from setting high tax rates?
validation_requirements: Simulate setting tax to 10001 and performing a transfer to verify underflow

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Complete destruction of the token economy

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All token holders and LP providers

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Check admin key security and timelocks
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify unchecked block behavior in Solidity 0.8+

threats.title: Founder Liquidity Lock Bypass via Admin
threats.hypothesis_description: The admin can arbitrarily bypass the founder's initial liquidity lock by setting the maturity timestamp to the past, allowing the founder to rug pull the initial liquidity.
threats.investigation_rationale: In `AgentVeToken.sol`, the founder's initial stake is locked until `matureAt`. However, the `setMatureAt` function allows an address with `ADMIN_ROLE` on the `agentNft` to change this timestamp to any value, including a timestamp in the past. If the admin colludes with the founder (or if the admin key is compromised), they can instantly unlock the founder's LP tokens, allowing the founder to withdraw and dump the initial liquidity, rug-pulling the stakers.

proof_of_signal.pattern: setMatureAt does not enforce newMatureAt >= oldMatureAt or block.timestamp

preconditions: Admin colludes with founder or admin key is compromised

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Admin sets matureAt to 0, bypassing the time-lock
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Founder extracts initial LP liquidity before the promised lock period expires

economic_questions: Is the admin role held by a multisig or a single EOA?
What is the standard maturity duration promised to users?
validation_requirements: Verify that setMatureAt can accept past timestamps

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Value of the initial LP deposit

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When the LP has accumulated maximum external value
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Rug pull of the agent token

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Initial LP liquidity + accumulated trading fees
